title: Market Discovery: GfS Türwächter (Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT)

sections.title: Phase 02a — Product-Agnostic NAICS Discovery
sections.content:
### Input Context

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Commodity / Custom Product Group** | Emergency Exit Door Alarm Guards |
| **Commodity Functional Promise** | Prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability and alerting to door activation via audible alarm |
| **FP Extension** | Monitor distributed safety-critical hardware wirelessly, detecting activation events and battery status without mains wiring at the device point, and aggregating status to a centralized building management system |
| **Discovery sources** | Primary FP (commodity-anchored) + FP Extension (cross-domain IoT monitoring) |
| **Excluded markets** | 332321 — Metal Window and Door Manufacturing (home market for Assa Abloy / GfS as manufacturer) |

### Discovery Method

Two parallel searches executed:

1. **Primary search** (Commodity FP): "What 6-digit NAICS industries need to prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability?" — targets all building types with mandated emergency exits.
2. **Secondary search** (FP Extension): "What 6-digit NAICS industries manage distributed passive safety-critical devices requiring wireless monitoring without mains power?" — targets adjacent safety-monitoring use cases.

Augmentation applied: broad sector sweep across healthcare, education, hospitality, transportation, government, retail, industrial, logistics, food service, cultural institutions, senior living, data infrastructure, and defense.

---

### Candidate Markets

#### Market 01 — General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 622110 |
| **NAICS title** | General Medical and Surgical Hospitals |
| **Relevance rationale** | Hospitals have dozens to hundreds of emergency exits across wings, floors, and departments. Many exit doors are in sensitive zones (psychiatric wards, neonatal units, pharmacy stores) where unauthorized egress or ingress creates patient safety and drug diversion risks. Fire code mandates escape routes remain fully functional at all times. Manual patrol of all exits is prohibitively expensive for 24/7 operations. |
| **Job to be done** | Secure restricted-zone emergency exits against unauthorized patient elopement or unauthorized access while maintaining fire-code-compliant escape routes |
| **Why commodity needed** | Hospitals must balance patient containment (psychiatric, dementia, pediatric) with fire safety evacuation compliance. Current solutions range from manual door checks to expensive wired electronic systems. Battery-powered wireless monitoring enables retrofit across legacy hospital wings without construction disruption. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Wired electronic access control (dormakaba, ASSA ABLOY), nurse-station door alarms, manual patrol rounds, CCTV-based monitoring |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.94 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$800M (hospital security systems, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 02 — Elementary and Secondary Schools

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 611110 |
| **NAICS title** | Elementary and Secondary Schools |
| **Relevance rationale** | Schools have multiple emergency exit doors that must remain unlocked from inside during occupied hours (fire code) but must be monitored to prevent unauthorized entry from outside (security against intruders) and unauthorized exit by students. Post-Uvalde legislation across US states and EU security directives have dramatically increased school door security spending. |
| **Job to be done** | Monitor all emergency exit doors for unauthorized opening during school hours while maintaining instant evacuation capability during emergencies |
| **Why commodity needed** | Schools operate under extreme dual pressure: fire marshals require unimpeded exits; security protocols require controlled access. Budget-constrained school districts need retrofit-friendly, low-maintenance solutions that do not require mains wiring to each door. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Mechanical-only door alarms (no monitoring), wired alarm panels, CCTV at exits, security personnel stationed at doors, door prop alarms |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.93 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$500M (school security hardware, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 03 — Hotels (except Casino Hotels) and Motels

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 721110 |
| **NAICS title** | Hotels (except Casino Hotels) and Motels |
| **Relevance rationale** | Hotels have emergency exits on every floor, in stairwells, and at ground level. Guests frequently misuse emergency exits (propping doors for smoking, unauthorized roof access, bypassing lobby). Each false alarm disrupts guest experience. Hotels need silent monitoring with centralized alerting rather than loud alarms that disturb guests on upper floors. The IoT dashboard for multi-property hotel chains is a strong value proposition. |
| **Job to be done** | Prevent guest misuse of emergency stairwell and fire exit doors while maintaining fire evacuation compliance across all floors and wings |
| **Why commodity needed** | Large hotels may have 50-100+ emergency exit points across floors. Manual patrol is impractical. Wired systems are cost-prohibitive for retrofit in existing properties. Wireless, battery-operated monitoring with centralized dashboards enables portfolio-wide visibility for hotel management companies. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Stairwell re-entry locks, mechanical door alarms, wired alarm systems integrated with property management, security guard patrols |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | established |
| **Confidence** | 0.92 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$350M (hotel security systems, global) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 04 — Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 531120 |
| **NAICS title** | Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings (except Miniwarehouses) |
| **Relevance rationale** | Commercial office building landlords are responsible for fire safety compliance across multi-tenant buildings. Emergency exits are shared infrastructure. Landlords must demonstrate compliance during insurance inspections and fire marshal audits. Multi-building portfolios need centralized monitoring across properties. Tenants expect the landlord to manage exit security without impeding their operations. |
| **Job to be done** | Maintain fire-code-compliant emergency exits across multi-tenant commercial buildings while monitoring for unauthorized use and generating audit-ready compliance documentation |
| **Why commodity needed** | Office building portfolios may span hundreds of emergency exit doors across multiple properties. Landlords need cost-effective, scalable monitoring that works in existing buildings without costly wiring retrofits. IoT dashboards aggregate status across the portfolio for centralized property management teams. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Basic mechanical door alarms, wired fire alarm panel integration, security guard patrols, tenant-managed access control |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.91 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$600M (commercial building safety systems, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 05 — Other Airport Operations

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 488119 |
| **NAICS title** | Other Airport Operations |
| **Relevance rationale** | Airports have extensive emergency exit infrastructure across terminals, concourses, jet bridges, cargo areas, and restricted airside zones. Security is paramount — unauthorized door opening in sterile areas triggers full security responses. Airports operate 24/7 with massive footprints where manual door monitoring is impossible. Regulatory requirements (TSA, EASA) mandate documented access control at all perimeter and zone-transition points. |
| **Job to be done** | Monitor all emergency exit doors across terminal zones, airside/landside boundaries, and restricted areas for unauthorized opening while maintaining evacuation readiness |
| **Why commodity needed** | Airports have hundreds of emergency exits, many in remote locations (cargo, maintenance hangars, underground service corridors). Wired systems exist for primary doors but secondary and tertiary exits are often under-monitored. Wireless retrofit devices can close coverage gaps cost-effectively. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Wired electronic access control (Lenel, Honeywell), CCTV monitoring, security patrols, alarmed crash bars, integrated airport security management systems |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | established |
| **Confidence** | 0.88 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$1.2B (airport security systems, global) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 06 — General Warehousing and Storage

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 493110 |
| **NAICS title** | General Warehousing and Storage |
| **Relevance rationale** | Large distribution centers and warehouses have multiple emergency exits across vast floor areas. Fire code mandates are strict due to high-value inventory and combustible packaging. Emergency exits are frequently propped open by workers for ventilation or convenience, creating security and liability risks. Facility managers need remote visibility into door status across shifts. |
| **Job to be done** | Prevent unauthorized propping or misuse of emergency exit doors in warehouse facilities while maintaining fire evacuation compliance for large-footprint buildings |
| **Why commodity needed** | Warehouses have expansive layouts with exits far from management offices. Manual monitoring is impractical across 3-shift operations. Battery-powered wireless monitoring is ideal because warehouse emergency exits are typically far from electrical infrastructure. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Door prop alarms, mechanical door alarms, wired alarm systems, security cameras at exits, guard patrols |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.87 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$250M (warehouse security hardware, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 07 — Continuing Care Retirement Communities

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 623311 |
| **NAICS title** | Continuing Care Retirement Communities |
| **Relevance rationale** | Senior living facilities face a critical safety challenge: residents with dementia or cognitive decline may attempt to leave through emergency exits (elopement), creating life-threatening situations. Simultaneously, fire codes mandate that these same exits remain usable for evacuation. This is the exact dual-mandate tension the commodity functional promise addresses. |
| **Job to be done** | Prevent cognitively impaired residents from eloping through emergency exits while maintaining instant evacuation capability for staff-assisted emergency egress |
| **Why commodity needed** | Elopement prevention is a major liability and regulatory concern in senior care. Current wired wander-management systems (RFID wristbands + door locks) are expensive and complex. A simpler, retrofit-ready alarm + wireless monitoring system provides a cost-effective layer of protection, especially for smaller facilities. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | RFID/BLE wander-management systems (Stanley Healthcare, CenTrak), delayed-egress electromagnetic locks, coded keypad exits, staff stationed at exits |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.90 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$400M (senior care security/wander prevention, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 08 — Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 611310 |
| **NAICS title** | Colleges, Universities, and Professional Schools |
| **Relevance rationale** | University campuses have dozens of buildings with hundreds of emergency exits across dormitories, lecture halls, laboratories, libraries, and administrative buildings. Campus security must balance open-campus culture with building security. Labs and research facilities have controlled-access requirements where emergency exits must be monitored for unauthorized entry. |
| **Job to be done** | Monitor emergency exit doors across distributed campus buildings for unauthorized access or exit while maintaining fire evacuation compliance for large, variable-occupancy buildings |
| **Why commodity needed** | Universities have distributed, aging building stock where wired alarm systems are expensive to retrofit. Wireless, battery-operated monitoring allows incremental deployment building-by-building. Centralized campus security dashboards provide real-time visibility across the entire estate. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Campus access card systems, wired door alarms, CCTV monitoring, campus police patrols, mechanical door alarms |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.86 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$300M (campus security hardware, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 09 — Facilities Support Services

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 561210 |
| **NAICS title** | Facilities Support Services |
| **Relevance rationale** | Facilities management companies operate and maintain buildings for clients across all sectors (office, healthcare, education, government). They are responsible for fire safety compliance, security system monitoring, and maintenance across large portfolios. FM companies are the procurement channel for building safety hardware — they specify, install, and maintain door alarm systems. |
| **Job to be done** | Efficiently manage fire safety compliance and emergency exit door monitoring across a portfolio of client buildings with minimal on-site labor |
| **Why commodity needed** | FM companies need standardized, low-maintenance solutions deployable across diverse building types. Wireless IoT monitoring with centralized dashboards aligns with their service model: remote monitoring reduces site visits, automated compliance logs reduce audit labor, and predictive battery replacement reduces emergency callouts. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Manual inspection rounds, wired alarm panels per building, paper-based compliance logs, third-party security monitoring services |
| **Functional promise fit** | direct |
| **Estimated adoption** | growing |
| **Confidence** | 0.89 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$450M (FM safety compliance services, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 10 — Refrigerated Warehousing and Storage

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 493120 |
| **NAICS title** | Refrigerated Warehousing and Storage |
| **Relevance rationale** | Cold storage facilities have emergency exits that must remain operable in extreme conditions (-25C to +5C). Unauthorized door opening causes temperature excursions that damage perishable inventory worth millions. Every door-open event must be logged for HACCP/food safety compliance. Real-time wireless monitoring of cold-room emergency exits is critical for both safety and inventory protection. |
| **Job to be done** | Monitor emergency exit and cold-room access doors for unauthorized opening to prevent temperature excursions while maintaining evacuation capability in extreme-temperature environments |
| **Why commodity needed** | Cold storage facilities need door monitoring that works in extreme cold (battery performance, mechanical reliability). Wired systems are difficult to install through insulated walls. Wireless monitoring with temperature-event correlation provides dual value: safety compliance AND cold-chain integrity. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Temperature-monitored door sensors (wired), mechanical door alarms, SCADA-integrated door contacts, manual inspection logs |
| **Functional promise fit** | adjacent |
| **Estimated adoption** | emerging |
| **Confidence** | 0.78 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$150M (cold storage door monitoring, global) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp + fp_extension |

#### Market 11 — Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 712110 |
| **NAICS title** | Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions |
| **Relevance rationale** | Museums have emergency exits throughout gallery spaces, storage vaults, and conservation areas. Unauthorized exit use can indicate theft attempts or artifact removal. Museums in historic buildings cannot modify door infrastructure (heritage conservation rules). Battery-powered, surface-mounted monitoring that requires zero structural modification is uniquely suited to heritage-listed buildings. |
| **Job to be done** | Protect exhibition and storage areas by monitoring emergency exits for unauthorized use while maintaining fire evacuation compliance in heritage-listed buildings that prohibit structural modification |
| **Why commodity needed** | Heritage building constraints make wired security systems difficult or impossible to install. Retrofit-ready, non-invasive wireless monitoring solves the heritage-vs-security tension. Centralized dashboards allow security teams to monitor exits across large museum complexes. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Guard patrols, CCTV, basic mechanical door alarms, gallery attendants, wired intrusion detection systems |
| **Functional promise fit** | adjacent |
| **Estimated adoption** | emerging |
| **Confidence** | 0.80 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$120M (museum security hardware, global) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 12 — Data Processing, Hosting, and Related Services

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 518210 |
| **NAICS title** | Computing Infrastructure Providers, Data Processing, Web Hosting, and Related Services |
| **Relevance rationale** | Data centers have strict physical security requirements with multiple controlled perimeter zones. Emergency exits must exist per fire code but each unauthorized opening is a potential security breach that can compromise SLA commitments and compliance certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001). Every door event must be logged with timestamps for audit trails. |
| **Job to be done** | Log and monitor every emergency exit door event across data center facilities for security compliance auditing while maintaining fire evacuation readiness |
| **Why commodity needed** | Data centers require documented door-event logs for compliance certifications. Wireless monitoring supplements wired access control on secondary exits and provides redundant monitoring. Battery-powered operation ensures monitoring continues during power events (when emergency exits are most critical). |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Enterprise access control systems (Honeywell, Lenel), biometric entry systems, mantrap configurations, CCTV with analytics |
| **Functional promise fit** | adjacent |
| **Estimated adoption** | established |
| **Confidence** | 0.82 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$200M (data center physical security, global) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp |

#### Market 13 — Pharmaceutical and Medicine Manufacturing

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 325411 |
| **NAICS title** | Medicinal and Botanical Manufacturing |
| **Relevance rationale** | Pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities operate clean rooms and controlled environments where every door opening is a contamination event. Emergency exits must exist per GMP and fire code but unauthorized opening compromises batch integrity. Real-time door status monitoring integrates with environmental monitoring systems required by FDA/EMA regulations. |
| **Job to be done** | Monitor clean-room and controlled-area emergency exit doors for unauthorized opening to prevent contamination while maintaining fire evacuation compliance per GMP and safety regulations |
| **Why commodity needed** | Pharma facilities need door-event logging integrated with batch records for regulatory compliance. Wireless monitoring avoids penetrating clean-room walls with wiring (which compromises pressure differentials). Battery-powered, surface-mounted devices avoid contamination risks of complex installations. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Clean-room-grade electronic access control, environmental monitoring systems with door contacts, GMP-compliant wired alarm panels, manual SOPs |
| **Functional promise fit** | adjacent |
| **Estimated adoption** | emerging |
| **Confidence** | 0.75 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$300M (pharma facility safety/access control, global) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp + fp_extension |

#### Market 14 — Fire Protection (Government)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 922160 |
| **NAICS title** | Fire Protection |
| **Relevance rationale** | Fire protection authorities are the regulatory enforcers and specifiers for emergency exit compliance. While not end-users of door hardware, fire marshals and fire prevention bureaus influence procurement decisions across all building types. IoT-enabled compliance reporting that generates audit-ready documentation directly addresses fire inspectors' verification needs. The FP Extension (wireless monitoring of distributed safety devices) maps directly to fire departments' inspection mandates for fire doors, smoke barriers, and emergency lighting. |
| **Job to be done** | Efficiently verify emergency exit compliance across jurisdiction buildings using remotely accessible, digitally documented inspection data rather than manual on-site checks |
| **Why commodity needed** | Fire inspectors must verify hundreds of buildings annually. Remote monitoring dashboards with automated compliance logs reduce inspection burden. IoT-enabled exit devices that self-report status represent a shift from periodic manual inspection to continuous compliance monitoring. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | Manual fire inspections, paper inspection forms, building-by-building walk-throughs, self-certification by building owners |
| **Functional promise fit** | adjacent |
| **Estimated adoption** | emerging |
| **Confidence** | 0.72 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$100M (fire inspection technology, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | fp_extension |

#### Market 15 — Food Manufacturing (Broad)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **NAICS code** | 311511 |
| **NAICS title** | Fluid Milk Manufacturing |
| **Relevance rationale** | Food manufacturing facilities operate under HACCP and food safety regulations that require controlled access to production areas. Emergency exits in production zones must be monitored because unauthorized opening can introduce contaminants, pest entry, or temperature excursions. FSMA (Food Safety Modernization Act) compliance increasingly requires documented access control logs. Representative of the broader food manufacturing sector (NAICS 311xxx) where similar requirements apply across dairy, meat, bakery, and beverage facilities. |
| **Job to be done** | Monitor production-area emergency exits for unauthorized opening to prevent contamination and maintain HACCP compliance while preserving fire evacuation capability |
| **Why commodity needed** | Food manufacturing lines operate continuously. Door-open events in production areas must be time-stamped and documented for food safety audits. Wireless monitoring avoids disrupting hygiene-sensitive production environments with wiring installations. |
| **Current alternatives hired** | HACCP-integrated door sensors, production-area access control, manual door checks per shift, SCADA-connected door contacts |
| **Functional promise fit** | adjacent |
| **Estimated adoption** | emerging |
| **Confidence** | 0.74 |
| **Estimated market size (USD)** | ~$180M (food manufacturing access/safety systems, US+EU) |
| **Discovery source** | primary_fp + fp_extension |

sections.title: Phase 02b — Architecture Distance Ranking
sections.content:
### Product Context (for distance scoring)

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Product name** | GfS Türwächter / Exit Control 179/1125 with IoT |
| **Technology class** | Emergency Exit Security Devices with Remote Monitoring |
| **Product FP** | Secure emergency exit doors against unauthorized use while preserving unimpeded single-motion escape capability and reporting door status events to centralized monitoring — without mains power at the door |
| **Mechanism** | Spring-loaded mechanical guard over door handle with piezo horn (95 dB) + 868 MHz EasyWave radio transmitter |
| **Key specs** | 95 dB alarm, 9V battery, 868.3 MHz, ~30 m wireless range, IP45 option, ~730 EUR (IoT variant) |
| **Constraints** | DIN EN 179 / EN 1125 compliance, mechanical-primary fail-safe, retrofit-only installation, EasyWave protocol lock-in |
| **Scoring type** | Hardware components (L1-L4) — using criteria U1-U5 + H1-H4 |

### Scoring Criteria Applied

**Universal (U1-U5):**
- U1: Operating environment similarity
- U2: Regulatory regime distance
- U3: Customer type similarity
- U4: Integration architecture
- U5: Price point alignment

**Hardware-specific (H1-H4):**
- H1: Physical envelope match (size, mounting, interfaces)
- H2: Media/material compatibility (what the product contacts)
- H3: Performance specification match (alarm, range, battery)
- H4: Certification transferability (EN 179/1125, CE)

### Tiered Discovery

Not activated — 15 candidates is below the 20-candidate threshold. All candidates receive full 9-criteria scoring.

---

### Ranked Markets (ascending by architecture distance)

#### Rank 1 — 531120 Lessors of Nonresidential Buildings

| Criterion | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| U1 Operating environment | 1 | Indoor commercial buildings — identical to current use |
| U2 Regulatory regime | 1 | Same EN 179/1125 fire codes; same building safety regulations |
| U3 Customer type | 2 | Property managers vs. building owners — adjacent but very similar buyer |
| U4 Integration architecture | 2 | Drop-in compatible; may need BMS gateway integration |
| U5 Price point | 2 | Same order of magnitude; portfolio volume drives unit cost sensitivity |
| H1 Physical envelope | 1 | Standard commercial doors, identical mounting |
| H2 Media/material | 1 | Standard door handles and panic bars |
| H3 Performance specs | 1 | 95 dB alarm, 30 m range fully adequate for office floors |
| H4 Certification | 1 | EN 179/1125 directly applies |

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **product_architecture_distance** | 1 |
| **already_uses_technology** | true |
| **market_priority** | HIGH |
| **architecture_rationale** | Nearly identical use case to the product's design intent. Commercial office buildings are the canonical deployment environment. EN 179/1125 certifications apply directly. No adaptation needed — product is drop-in ready. The only delta is BMS integration, which the existing wireless BUS interface already supports. |

#### Rank 2 — 721110 Hotels (except Casino Hotels) and Motels

| Criterion | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| U1 Operating environment | 1 | Indoor, climate-controlled — identical |
| U2 Regulatory regime | 1 | Same fire codes apply; hospitality may add local hospitality safety codes |
| U3 Customer type | 2 | Hotel facility managers are similar to office building managers |
| U4 Integration architecture | 3 | Hotels may want property management system (PMS) integration beyond BMS |
| U5 Price point | 2 | Hotels budget for safety hardware; 50-100 doors per property scales within range |
| H1 Physical envelope | 1 | Standard hotel doors, fire-rated stairwell doors — identical |
| H2 Media/material | 1 | Standard door hardware |
| H3 Performance specs | 2 | May need quieter pre-alarm for guest comfort; alarm volume configurable |
| H4 Certification | 1 | EN 179/1125 applies directly |

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **product_architecture_distance** | 2 |
| **already_uses_technology** | true |
| **market_priority** | HIGH |
| **architecture_rationale** | Hotels are a near-perfect fit. Fire stairwell doors and emergency exits are architecturally identical to the product's design target. The only adaptation is potential PMS integration (property management system) and noise-sensitivity considerations for guest floors, both addressable with existing configurable alarm volume and standard gateway integration. |

#### Rank 3 — 622110 General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

| Criterion | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| U1 Operating environment | 2 | Indoor, but includes sterile and semi-sterile zones; some outdoor-adjacent exits |
| U2 Regulatory regime | 3 | Healthcare adds HIPAA (if door logs identify patients), JCI accreditation, additional safety standards beyond fire codes |
| U3 Customer type | 3 | Hospital facility management is specialized; procurement involves clinical and administrative stakeholders |
| U4 Integration architecture | 3 | Hospitals use specialized BMS (Siemens Desigo, Honeywell EBI); integration expectations higher |
| U5 Price point | 2 | Hospital budgets accommodate safety hardware; volume per facility is high |
| H1 Physical envelope | 1 | Standard fire-rated hospital doors |
| H2 Media/material | 2 | May need antimicrobial housing in clinical areas |
| H3 Performance specs | 2 | Alarm volume may need adjustment near patient areas; range adequate for ward-level coverage |
| H4 Certification | 2 | EN 179/1125 applies; may need additional healthcare facility certifications |

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **product_architecture_distance** | 2 |
| **already_uses_technology** | true |
| **market_priority** | HIGH |
| **architecture_rationale** | Hospitals are a strong fit with minor adaptations. The core product works on standard hospital fire doors. Additional requirements include potential antimicrobial housing options, quieter alarm profiles near patient areas, and integration with hospital-grade BMS platforms. EN 179/1125 certifications transfer directly. The high door count per hospital (50-200+) makes the IoT dashboard especially valuable. |

#### Rank 4 — 611110 Elementary and Secondary Schools

| Criterion | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| U1 Operating environment | 1 | Indoor, standard building conditions |
| U2 Regulatory regime | 2 | Same fire codes; schools add education-specific safety regulations and lockdown protocols |
| U3 Customer type | 3 | School districts have unique procurement processes (public procurement, budget cycles) |
| U4 Integration architecture | 2 | Schools have simpler BMS; direct-to-dashboard integration sufficient |
| U5 Price point | 4 | Schools are severely budget-constrained; ~730 EUR per door is high for public education budgets |
| H1 Physical envelope | 1 | Standard school doors |
| H2 Media/material | 1 | Standard door hardware |
| H3 Performance specs | 2 | Alarm volume appropriate; wireless range covers typical school corridor distances |
| H4 Certification | 1 | EN 179/1125 applies directly |

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **product_architecture_distance** | 2 |
| **already_uses_technology** | true |
| **market_priority** | HIGH |
| **architecture_rationale** | Schools are an excellent functional fit — the product installs without modification on standard school doors. The primary friction is price sensitivity in public education budgets. Lockdown integration (alarm silence during lockdown drills) may require firmware adaptation. The retrofit capability is especially valuable in aging school buildings. |

#### Rank 5 — 623311 Continuing Care Retirement Communities

| Criterion | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| U1 Operating environment | 1 | Indoor, residential-style building |
| U2 Regulatory regime | 3 | Senior care adds elopement-prevention regulations, state licensing requirements |
| U3 Customer type | 3 | Senior care facility operators; procurement involves clinical and operations teams |
| U4 Integration architecture | 3 | May need integration with nurse-call systems and wander-management platforms |
| U5 Price point | 2 | Senior care facilities budget for safety; fewer doors than hospitals |
| H1 Physical envelope | 1 | Standard residential-commercial doors |
| H2 Media/material | 1 | Standard door hardware |
| H3 Performance specs | 2 | Alarm at doors; wireless monitoring to nurse stations is core value |
| H4 Certification | 3 | EN 179/1125 applies but elopement-prevention standards (varies by jurisdiction) add requirements |

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **product_architecture_distance** | 2 |
| **already_uses_technology** | true |
| **market_priority** | HIGH |
| **architecture_rationale** | The elopement-prevention use case in senior care is a near-perfect match for the Türwächter's dual mandate: prevent unauthorized exit while maintaining escape capability. The product works on standard doors without modification. Integration with nurse-call systems and wander-management platforms would require gateway/API work but no hardware changes. |

#### Rank 6 — 561210 Facilities Support Services

| Criterion | Score | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| U1 Operating environment | 2 | Varies by client facility — FM companies serve diverse building types |
| U2 Regulatory regime | 2 | Must comply with fire codes of each client building type |

### Phase 02b Summary — Final Ranking

| Rank | NAICS | Title | Distance | FP Fit | Uses Tech | Priority |

| 5 | 623311 | Continuing Care Retirement Communities | 2 | direct | Yes | HIGH |

| 14 | 311511 | Fluid Milk Manufacturing | 4 | adjacent | No | LOW |
| 15 | 325411 | Medicinal and Botanical Manufacturing | 5 | adjacent | No | LOW |

### Key Observations

1. **Eight HIGH-priority markets** all score distance 1-2, meaning the product is drop-in ready or requires only minor adaptation (BMS integration, alarm volume profiles). These represent the immediate addressable market.
2. **The strongest cluster** is building-centric: commercial offices, hotels, hospitals, schools, senior care, universities, and warehouses. All share standard EN 179/1125 compliant doors where the product mounts without modification.
3. **Facilities Support Services (561210)** is a force-multiplier channel — selling through FM companies reaches all building types simultaneously.
4. **Museums (712110)** are a compelling niche because the product's non-invasive, retrofit-ready design directly solves the heritage building constraint that blocks competitors requiring wired installation.
5. **Data centers (518210)** need the function but demand IP-native protocols — the EasyWave proprietary protocol is the main barrier.
6. **Industrial markets** (pharma, dairy, cold storage) require new product variants (clean-room housing, IP65+, lithium batteries for cold) — these are product development opportunities, not immediate sales opportunities.
7. **Fire protection authorities (922160)** are an influence market that can accelerate adoption across all building types if IoT compliance logs are accepted as equivalent to physical inspections.

rows.Value: Prevent unauthorized use of emergency exit doors while maintaining unimpeded escape capability and alerting to door activation via audible alarm

Monitor distributed safety-critical hardware wirelessly, detecting activation events and battery status without mains wiring at the device point, and aggregating status to a centralized building management system
Primary FP (commodity-anchored) + FP Extension (cross-domain IoT monitoring)
332321 — Metal Window and Door Manufacturing (home market for Assa Abloy / GfS as manufacturer)

Hospitals have dozens to hundreds of emergency exits across wings, floors, and departments. Many exit doors are in sensitive zones (psychiatric wards, neonatal units, pharmacy stores) where unauthorized egress or ingress creates patient safety and drug diversion risks. Fire code mandates escape routes remain fully functional at all times. Manual patrol of all exits is prohibitively expensive for 24/7 operations.
Secure restricted-zone emergency exits against unauthorized patient elopement or unauthorized access while maintaining fire-code-compliant escape routes

Hospitals must balance patient containment (psychiatric, dementia, pediatric) with fire safety evacuation compliance. Current solutions range from manual door checks to expensive wired electronic systems. Battery-powered wireless monitoring enables retrofit across legacy hospital wings without construction disruption.
Wired electronic access control (dormakaba, ASSA ABLOY), nurse-station door alarms, manual patrol rounds, CCTV-based monitoring

Schools have multiple emergency exit doors that must remain unlocked from inside during occupied hours (fire code) but must be monitored to prevent unauthorized entry from outside (security against intruders) and unauthorized exit by students. Post-Uvalde legislation across US states and EU security directives have dramatically increased school door security spending.
Monitor all emergency exit doors for unauthorized opening during school hours while maintaining instant evacuation capability during emergencies

Schools operate under extreme dual pressure: fire marshals require unimpeded exits; security protocols require controlled access. Budget-constrained school districts need retrofit-friendly, low-maintenance solutions that do not require mains wiring to each door.
Mechanical-only door alarms (no monitoring), wired alarm panels, CCTV at exits, security personnel stationed at doors, door prop alarms

Hotels have emergency exits on every floor, in stairwells, and at ground level. Guests frequently misuse emergency exits (propping doors for smoking, unauthorized roof access, bypassing lobby). Each false alarm disrupts guest experience. Hotels need silent monitoring with centralized alerting rather than loud alarms that disturb guests on upper floors. The IoT dashboard for multi-property hotel chains is a strong value proposition.
Prevent guest misuse of emergency stairwell and fire exit doors while maintaining fire evacuation compliance across all floors and wings

Large hotels may have 50-100+ emergency exit points across floors. Manual patrol is impractical. Wired systems are cost-prohibitive for retrofit in existing properties. Wireless, battery-operated monitoring with centralized dashboards enables portfolio-wide visibility for hotel management companies.
Stairwell re-entry locks, mechanical door alarms, wired alarm systems integrated with property management, security guard patrols

Commercial office building landlords are responsible for fire safety compliance across multi-tenant buildings. Emergency exits are shared infrastructure. Landlords must demonstrate compliance during insurance inspections and fire marshal audits. Multi-building portfolios need centralized monitoring across properties. Tenants expect the landlord to manage exit security without impeding their operations.
Maintain fire-code-compliant emergency exits across multi-tenant commercial buildings while monitoring for unauthorized use and generating audit-ready compliance documentation

Office building portfolios may span hundreds of emergency exit doors across multiple properties. Landlords need cost-effective, scalable monitoring that works in existing buildings without costly wiring retrofits. IoT dashboards aggregate status across the portfolio for centralized property management teams.
Basic mechanical door alarms, wired fire alarm panel integration, security guard patrols, tenant-managed access control

Airports have extensive emergency exit infrastructure across terminals, concourses, jet bridges, cargo areas, and restricted airside zones. Security is paramount — unauthorized door opening in sterile areas triggers full security responses. Airports operate 24/7 with massive footprints where manual door monitoring is impossible. Regulatory requirements (TSA, EASA) mandate documented access control at all perimeter and zone-transition points.
Monitor all emergency exit doors across terminal zones, airside/landside boundaries, and restricted areas for unauthorized opening while maintaining evacuation readiness

Airports have hundreds of emergency exits, many in remote locations (cargo, maintenance hangars, underground service corridors). Wired systems exist for primary doors but secondary and tertiary exits are often under-monitored. Wireless retrofit devices can close coverage gaps cost-effectively.
Wired electronic access control (Lenel, Honeywell), CCTV monitoring, security patrols, alarmed crash bars, integrated airport security management systems

Large distribution centers and warehouses have multiple emergency exits across vast floor areas. Fire code mandates are strict due to high-value inventory and combustible packaging. Emergency exits are frequently propped open by workers for ventilation or convenience, creating security and liability risks. Facility managers need remote visibility into door status across shifts.
Prevent unauthorized propping or misuse of emergency exit doors in warehouse facilities while maintaining fire evacuation compliance for large-footprint buildings

Warehouses have expansive layouts with exits far from management offices. Manual monitoring is impractical across 3-shift operations. Battery-powered wireless monitoring is ideal because warehouse emergency exits are typically far from electrical infrastructure.
Door prop alarms, mechanical door alarms, wired alarm systems, security cameras at exits, guard patrols

Senior living facilities face a critical safety challenge: residents with dementia or cognitive decline may attempt to leave through emergency exits (elopement), creating life-threatening situations. Simultaneously, fire codes mandate that these same exits remain usable for evacuation. This is the exact dual-mandate tension the commodity functional promise addresses.
Prevent cognitively impaired residents from eloping through emergency exits while maintaining instant evacuation capability for staff-assisted emergency egress

Elopement prevention is a major liability and regulatory concern in senior care. Current wired wander-management systems (RFID wristbands + door locks) are expensive and complex. A simpler, retrofit-ready alarm + wireless monitoring system provides a cost-effective layer of protection, especially for smaller facilities.
RFID/BLE wander-management systems (Stanley Healthcare, CenTrak), delayed-egress electromagnetic locks, coded keypad exits, staff stationed at exits

University campuses have dozens of buildings with hundreds of emergency exits across dormitories, lecture halls, laboratories, libraries, and administrative buildings. Campus security must balance open-campus culture with building security. Labs and research facilities have controlled-access requirements where emergency exits must be monitored for unauthorized entry.
Monitor emergency exit doors across distributed campus buildings for unauthorized access or exit while maintaining fire evacuation compliance for large, variable-occupancy buildings

Universities have distributed, aging building stock where wired alarm systems are expensive to retrofit. Wireless, battery-operated monitoring allows incremental deployment building-by-building. Centralized campus security dashboards provide real-time visibility across the entire estate.
Campus access card systems, wired door alarms, CCTV monitoring, campus police patrols, mechanical door alarms

Facilities management companies operate and maintain buildings for clients across all sectors (office, healthcare, education, government). They are responsible for fire safety compliance, security system monitoring, and maintenance across large portfolios. FM companies are the procurement channel for building safety hardware — they specify, install, and maintain door alarm systems.
Efficiently manage fire safety compliance and emergency exit door monitoring across a portfolio of client buildings with minimal on-site labor

FM companies need standardized, low-maintenance solutions deployable across diverse building types. Wireless IoT monitoring with centralized dashboards aligns with their service model: remote monitoring reduces site visits, automated compliance logs reduce audit labor, and predictive battery replacement reduces emergency callouts.
Manual inspection rounds, wired alarm panels per building, paper-based compliance logs, third-party security monitoring services

Cold storage facilities have emergency exits that must remain operable in extreme conditions (-25C to +5C). Unauthorized door opening causes temperature excursions that damage perishable inventory worth millions. Every door-open event must be logged for HACCP/food safety compliance. Real-time wireless monitoring of cold-room emergency exits is critical for both safety and inventory protection.
Monitor emergency exit and cold-room access doors for unauthorized opening to prevent temperature excursions while maintaining evacuation capability in extreme-temperature environments

Cold storage facilities need door monitoring that works in extreme cold (battery performance, mechanical reliability). Wired systems are difficult to install through insulated walls. Wireless monitoring with temperature-event correlation provides dual value: safety compliance AND cold-chain integrity.
Temperature-monitored door sensors (wired), mechanical door alarms, SCADA-integrated door contacts, manual inspection logs
Museums, Historical Sites, and Similar Institutions

Museums have emergency exits throughout gallery spaces, storage vaults, and conservation areas. Unauthorized exit use can indicate theft attempts or artifact removal. Museums in historic buildings cannot modify door infrastructure (heritage conservation rules). Battery-powered, surface-mounted monitoring that requires zero structural modification is uniquely suited to heritage-listed buildings.
Protect exhibition and storage areas by monitoring emergency exits for unauthorized use while maintaining fire evacuation compliance in heritage-listed buildings that prohibit structural modification

Heritage building constraints make wired security systems difficult or impossible to install. Retrofit-ready, non-invasive wireless monitoring solves the heritage-vs-security tension. Centralized dashboards allow security teams to monitor exits across large museum complexes.
Guard patrols, CCTV, basic mechanical door alarms, gallery attendants, wired intrusion detection systems
Computing Infrastructure Providers, Data Processing, Web Hosting, and Related Services

Data centers have strict physical security requirements with multiple controlled perimeter zones. Emergency exits must exist per fire code but each unauthorized opening is a potential security breach that can compromise SLA commitments and compliance certifications (SOC 2, ISO 27001). Every door event must be logged with timestamps for audit trails.
Log and monitor every emergency exit door event across data center facilities for security compliance auditing while maintaining fire evacuation readiness

Data centers require documented door-event logs for compliance certifications. Wireless monitoring supplements wired access control on secondary exits and provides redundant monitoring. Battery-powered operation ensures monitoring continues during power events (when emergency exits are most critical).
Enterprise access control systems (Honeywell, Lenel), biometric entry systems, mantrap configurations, CCTV with analytics

Pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities operate clean rooms and controlled environments where every door opening is a contamination event. Emergency exits must exist per GMP and fire code but unauthorized opening compromises batch integrity. Real-time door status monitoring integrates with environmental monitoring systems required by FDA/EMA regulations.
Monitor clean-room and controlled-area emergency exit doors for unauthorized opening to prevent contamination while maintaining fire evacuation compliance per GMP and safety regulations

Pharma facilities need door-event logging integrated with batch records for regulatory compliance. Wireless monitoring avoids penetrating clean-room walls with wiring (which compromises pressure differentials). Battery-powered, surface-mounted devices avoid contamination risks of complex installations.
Clean-room-grade electronic access control, environmental monitoring systems with door contacts, GMP-compliant wired alarm panels, manual SOPs

Fire protection authorities are the regulatory enforcers and specifiers for emergency exit compliance. While not end-users of door hardware, fire marshals and fire prevention bureaus influence procurement decisions across all building types. IoT-enabled compliance reporting that generates audit-ready documentation directly addresses fire inspectors' verification needs. The FP Extension (wireless monitoring of distributed safety devices) maps directly to fire departments' inspection mandates for fire doors, smoke barriers, and emergency lighting.
Efficiently verify emergency exit compliance across jurisdiction buildings using remotely accessible, digitally documented inspection data rather than manual on-site checks

Fire inspectors must verify hundreds of buildings annually. Remote monitoring dashboards with automated compliance logs reduce inspection burden. IoT-enabled exit devices that self-report status represent a shift from periodic manual inspection to continuous compliance monitoring.
Manual fire inspections, paper inspection forms, building-by-building walk-throughs, self-certification by building owners

Food manufacturing facilities operate under HACCP and food safety regulations that require controlled access to production areas. Emergency exits in production zones must be monitored because unauthorized opening can introduce contaminants, pest entry, or temperature excursions. FSMA (Food Safety Modernization Act) compliance increasingly requires documented access control logs. Representative of the broader food manufacturing sector (NAICS 311xxx) where similar requirements apply across dairy, meat, bakery, and beverage facilities.
Monitor production-area emergency exits for unauthorized opening to prevent contamination and maintain HACCP compliance while preserving fire evacuation capability

Food manufacturing lines operate continuously. Door-open events in production areas must be time-stamped and documented for food safety audits. Wireless monitoring avoids disrupting hygiene-sensitive production environments with wiring installations.
HACCP-integrated door sensors, production-area access control, manual door checks per shift, SCADA-connected door contacts
